question: What is 3D Gaussian Splatting and what makes it real-time?
answer:
## 3D Gaussian Splatting

**3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)** is a scene representation and rendering method for **novel-view synthesis**. Instead of modeling a scene as an implicit radiance field that must be sampled along rays, it represents the scene as a collection of **anisotropic 3D Gaussians**. Each Gaussian stores a **position**, **full 3×3 covariance**, **opacity**, and **view-dependent color encoded with spherical harmonics**. These Gaussians are then rendered with **differentiable rasterization** rather than ray marching. [3]

This matters because 3DGS replaces the expensive implicit-volume rendering used by NeRF-style methods with an **explicit primitive-based pipeline**. In the original formulation, projected Gaussians are sorted and rendered **front-to-back** in a tile-based rasterization process, which avoids wasting computation on empty regions of space. That is the core reason it became a strong alternative to NeRF for static-scene rendering and novel-view synthesis. [3]

## What Makes It Real-Time

The context identifies **three design choices** that make 3DGS practical for real-time rendering at high resolution. First, optimization starts from a **sparse Structure-from-Motion point cloud**, which places Gaussians near the true scene surface from the beginning. Second, training uses an **interleaved densification and pruning schedule**, so primitives are cloned, split, or removed as needed instead of keeping a fixed inefficient representation. Third, the method uses a **fast visibility-aware tile rasterizer** with a GPU-friendly closed-form backward pass, which accelerates both training and inference. [3]

Because of this design, the method achieves **state-of-the-art quality while sustaining at least 30 FPS at 1080p** on unbounded scenes. The official repository description reinforces that the practical system includes the reference CUDA kernels for **differentiable Gaussian rasterization**, **adaptive density control**, and the **spherical-harmonics color model**, and that the training pipeline densifies a sparse COLMAP/SfM point cloud into millions of anisotropic 3D Gaussians. [3][19]

A useful low-level explanation is that the gsplat mathematical supplement breaks the pipeline into projection of 3D Gaussians into screen space, **tile-based rasterization**, **front-to-back alpha compositing**, and the associated gradients. This confirms that the real-time behavior comes from a rendering pipeline built around efficient rasterization of explicit primitives, rather than repeated dense sampling of an implicit field. [13]

question: How does 3D Gaussian Splatting differ from NeRF?
answer:
## How 3D Gaussian Splatting differs from NeRF

**3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)** and **NeRF** differ mainly in **scene representation** and **rendering strategy**.

NeRF represents a scene as an **implicit radiance field**, typically modeled by a neural network and rendered by **sampling and ray marching through a volume** along each camera ray. In contrast, 3DGS represents a scene explicitly as a **collection of anisotropic 3D Gaussians**, where each primitive has a position, covariance, opacity, and view-dependent color, and renders them through **differentiable rasterization** rather than volumetric ray marching. This lets 3DGS avoid spending computation on empty space along rays. [13]

A second major difference is **speed and real-time practicality**. The context states that NeRF-style methods must sample through empty regions of an implicit volume, while 3DGS instead performs a **tile-based, front-to-back sort of projected Gaussians**. Combined with initialization from a sparse point cloud, interleaved densification and pruning, and a visibility-aware rasterizer, this makes 3DGS practical for **real-time 1080p rendering**, with the paper reporting **at least 30 FPS** on unbounded scenes. [13]

There is also a difference in how the field views them conceptually. The context describes 3DGS as a major alternative that, in static-scene settings, **displaced NeRF as the dominant baseline** because it preserved high visual quality while rendering much faster. A daily digest in the corpus explicitly contrasts 3DGS with **Instant-NGP** and, by extension, the broader NeRF family: splatting replaces **implicit-volume ray marching** with **explicit anisotropic primitives rasterized front-to-back**. [11][13]

At the same time, 3DGS has its own tradeoffs. While it is described as **excellent for novel-view synthesis**, the corpus also says that vanilla 3DGS is **less suitable when accurate geometry is required** because its volumetric Gaussians are not view-consistent surfaces. This is one reason follow-on methods such as **2D Gaussian Splatting** and **SuGaR** were introduced to improve geometry extraction, whereas NeRF-related methods often relied on different surface-recovery pipelines. [5][7]

In short, the core distinction is:

- **NeRF**: implicit field + ray-marched volume rendering
- **3DGS**: explicit Gaussian primitives + rasterization-based splatting

According to the provided context, that shift is what gives 3DGS its strong advantage in **rendering efficiency** while keeping high-quality **novel-view synthesis**. [13]

question: How does NeRF represent a 3D scene and what are its main limitations?
answer:
## How NeRF Represents a 3D Scene

NeRF represents a 3D scene as a **continuous radiance field** parameterized by a **fully connected coordinate-based MLP**. The model takes a **5D input** consisting of a **3D spatial location** and a **2D viewing direction**, and predicts **volume density** and **view-dependent emitted radiance**. In practice, this lets NeRF act as the scene’s volumetric representation, so new images can be rendered by applying **volume rendering** along camera rays through the field. This formulation is the core reason NeRF became a foundational method for **novel view synthesis**. [1][6]

NeRF is trained from **posed multi-view images** of a single scene. The original reference implementation describes it as a simple network optimized to reproduce the input views using a rendering loss, with standard components such as **positional encoding** and **hierarchical volume sampling**. The canonical `bmild/nerf` implementation became the standard baseline and evaluation harness for many later NeRF variants and follow-up methods. [1][6]

## Main Limitations

A major limitation is that NeRF is primarily designed for **static scenes with consistent appearance across views** and usually assumes **accurate camera poses** from an upstream structure-from-motion pipeline. This makes it less suitable when pose estimation is unreliable or when the scene changes over time. [1]

NeRF is also weak at producing **clean explicit geometry**. The context notes that it often learns a **fuzzy volumetric density** rather than a sharp surface, and radiance fields more broadly are described as being excellent for view synthesis but **poor at directly producing a mesh**. While geometry can be extracted afterward, that is not NeRF’s native strength. [1]

Another limitation is efficiency. The original NeRF requires substantial optimization time: the reference README states that training can take **hours to days per scene**, and rendering can still take from **less than a second to around 30 seconds per image**, depending on resolution. Later work in the corpus positions faster methods such as **Instant-NGP** and **Plenoxels** as major improvements over this baseline. [1][9]

NeRF also has a **sampling and aliasing problem**. Because each pixel is rendered by tracing a single infinitesimally thin ray, it can produce artifacts when training and test images observe the scene at different resolutions. This weakness motivated follow-up methods such as **Mip-NeRF**. [9]

Finally, conventional NeRF fails in **monocular dynamic-scene settings** because there are not enough simultaneous viewpoints to disentangle **motion from parallax**. That limitation is one reason later dynamic methods such as **Nerfies** and **Dynamic View Synthesis from Dynamic Monocular Video** introduce deformation fields or static/dynamic decompositions. [2][14]

question: How does Instant-NGP achieve such fast NeRF training?
answer:
## How Instant-NGP achieves fast NeRF training

Instant-NGP is described as achieving its speed from **two main components working together**:

1. **A multiresolution hash grid encoding**
2. **A tiny MLP implemented with fully fused CUDA kernels / the tiny-CUDA-NN fused runtime**

The core idea is that, instead of forcing a larger network to learn scene detail inefficiently, Instant-NGP maps 3D coordinates through a **multi-resolution hash table** and feeds those encoded features into a **small MLP**. At each resolution level, nearby grid-corner features are looked up, interpolated, and concatenated before entering the network. This gives the model an efficient spatial representation, so the MLP has much less work to do than in earlier NeRF-style approaches. [7]

The implementation side is equally important. The system is described as being built **end-to-end in fully fused CUDA kernels**, with the **tiny-CUDA-NN fused MLP runtime** specifically identified as one of the pieces that helped reduce NeRF training time from **hours to seconds**. In the repository summary, the combination of the **multiresolution hash grid encoding** and the **tiny-CUDA-NN fused MLP runtime** is explicitly credited with bringing NeRF training time down from hours to seconds. [3][7]

In the corpus, Instant-NGP is therefore presented as the clearest synthesis of the “fast NeRF” line: a **small network**, a **better coordinate encoding**, and a **highly optimized GPU implementation**. The result is described as training **from scratch in seconds on a single GPU**, with **combined speedups of several orders of magnitude** over both **NeRF** and **Mip-NeRF** on comparable hardware. [6][7]

For contrast, the original NeRF implementation is described as using a **5D radiance field MLP**, positional encoding, and hierarchical sampling, with optimization taking **hours to a day or two** for a single scene. Instant-NGP’s speed comes from replacing that much slower practical regime with a far more efficient encoding-and-runtime design. [2][4]

question: What is Score Distillation Sampling and how does DreamFusion use it?
answer:
## Score Distillation Sampling

**Score Distillation Sampling (SDS)** is the core optimization objective introduced by **DreamFusion** for turning a pretrained **2D text-to-image diffusion model** into a training signal for **3D generation**. In the provided context, SDS is described as DreamFusion’s main technical contribution: it uses the diffusion model’s predicted denoising direction as a gradient signal, so a 3D representation can be optimized toward images that the diffusion model considers likely under a given text prompt. DreamFusion’s significance is that it does this **without 3D training data** and **without modifying the diffusion model itself**.

Conceptually, SDS lets DreamFusion treat text-to-3D as an **optimization problem against an existing 2D image prior** rather than as a supervised 3D learning problem. Later work in the corpus also treats DreamFusion as the paper that established SDS as the foundational primitive for diffusion-based 3D generation.

## How DreamFusion Uses SDS

DreamFusion starts from a **Neural Radiance Field (NeRF)** initialized with random weights. For each optimization step, it:

1. Samples a random camera view.
2. Renders the current NeRF into a 2D image.
3. Adds Gaussian noise to that rendered image.
4. Feeds the noised image to the pretrained text-to-image diffusion model.
5. Uses the model’s predicted denoising direction as a gradient that is backpropagated through the renderer into the NeRF.

This process pushes each rendered view toward “looking like the prompt,” while different camera views are all constrained by the same prompt-conditioned prior. That is how DreamFusion obtains a **view-consistent 3D representation** from a 2D diffusion model.

## What SDS Achieves and Why It Matters

Within DreamFusion, SDS is the mechanism that connects a **2D diffusion prior** to a **3D scene optimization loop**. The result is a method that can generate 3D content from natural-language prompts using only the prior knowledge already present in the 2D diffusion model. The context also describes DreamFusion as the work that seeded later methods such as **Magic3D**, **ProlificDreamer**, and **Zero-1-to-3**.

The corpus also notes an important limitation: vanilla SDS later became known for issues such as **over-saturation, over-smoothing, and collapse to low-diversity modes**, which later work like **ProlificDreamer** explicitly analyzes and tries to fix.

question: What does Zero-1-to-3 enable that earlier image-to-3D methods couldn't?
answer:
## What Zero-1-to-3 Enabled

Zero-1-to-3 enabled **controllable novel-view synthesis and single-view 3D reconstruction from just one RGB image in a zero-shot setting**, which earlier image-to-3D methods in the context did not provide in the same way.

A key change was that Zero-1-to-3 **fine-tuned a Stable Diffusion model to be viewpoint-conditional**. Instead of only producing plausible imagery, it learned to generate a target view given **one input image plus a relative camera transformation**. That made it possible to rotate an object from a single image and synthesize consistent new viewpoints, rather than relying on category-specific models or producing views that were only visually plausible but geometrically inconsistent. The paper explicitly frames this as addressing the severely under-constrained **single-view-to-3D problem**. [2]

This viewpoint-conditioned model also enabled a second capability: using the learned model as a **diffusion prior for Score Distillation Sampling (SDS)** to optimize a **full 3D NeRF** from one image. In the context, this is described as producing a full 3D model that was **significantly more accurate than prior single-view 3D methods**. So Zero-1-to-3 did not just generate images of new views; it turned those geometric priors into a usable mechanism for **lifting a single image into 3D reconstruction**. [2][4]

More broadly, Zero-1-to-3 enabled a new bridge between research lines. The context says it **connected the text-to-3D line** represented by works like DreamFusion and Magic3D to the **single-image-to-3D line**, and that later methods such as **Magic123, DreamGaussian, SyncDreamer, Wonder3D, and One-2-3-45** built on this formulation or depended on its checkpoints and scripts. That means Zero-1-to-3 enabled not just a new capability in isolation, but a reusable **3D-aware image-conditioned prior** that later systems could adopt as infrastructure. [2][4][14]

In short, what Zero-1-to-3 enabled beyond earlier methods was:

- **Zero-shot generalization** from synthetic training to in-the-wild inputs such as photos, sketches, and paintings. [2]
- **Viewpoint-conditioned novel view synthesis** from a single image. [2][4]
- **Single-image 3D reconstruction via SDS-guided lifting to NeRF**, using the same model as a geometric prior. [2][4]
- A practical foundation for later **image-conditioned 3D generation pipelines** across the field. [4][14]

question: How does 4D Gaussian Splatting extend 3DGS to dynamic scenes?
answer:
## How 4D Gaussian Splatting Extends 3DGS to Dynamic Scenes

**4D Gaussian Splatting (4D-GS)** extends **3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)** from **static scenes** to **time-varying scenes** by keeping a **shared canonical set of 3D Gaussians** and adding a **compact spatio-temporal deformation field** that deforms those Gaussians at each timestamp. Instead of building a separate 3DGS model for every frame, the method reuses the same Gaussians across time and predicts how they move or change as a function of the temporal coordinate. This avoids the waste of per-frame reconstruction when much of a video is locally static. [1][3]

The deformation field is implemented in a **HexPlane-style decomposed neural voxel grid**. According to the context, this grid uses planar projections and feature planes to encode the 4D function efficiently, and a lightweight MLP decodes the retrieved features into **per-Gaussian deformations of position, rotation, and scale**. In this way, 4D-GS preserves the explicit Gaussian primitive structure of 3DGS while introducing temporal variation through a learned deformation mechanism. [1]

This design matters because it keeps rendering efficient. The context states that, since the canonical Gaussians remain shared and the deformation network is small, the runtime stays **close to static 3DGS**, enabling **real-time rendering of moving content from monocular video**. The method is described as achieving competitive or better reconstruction quality than prior dynamic NeRF approaches while sustaining real-time frame rates. [1][3]

Another key extension is in **storage and scalability over time**. Because the Gaussians are shared across all frames, memory usage scales mainly with the **deformation grid** rather than linearly with sequence length. This makes the approach more tractable for multi-second dynamic captures on consumer GPUs than storing a separate Gaussian representation per frame would be. [1]

question: How does DROID-SLAM differ from classical SLAM systems?
answer:
## How DROID-SLAM Differs from Classical SLAM Systems

DROID-SLAM differs from classical SLAM systems primarily in **how it performs tracking and optimization**. According to the provided context, it replaces the **hand-engineered front end** used by classical pipelines such as **ORB-SLAM** and **DSO** with a **recurrent neural network**, while keeping optimization as a central part of the system through a **differentiable Dense Bundle Adjustment (DBA) layer**. In other words, the feature extraction and update mechanism are learned, rather than manually designed. This makes DROID-SLAM a **deep visual SLAM** system rather than a purely classical geometry-driven one. [5][6]

Architecturally, DROID-SLAM combines **recurrent optical-flow updates** with a **dense bundle adjustment layer**. Its update operator inspects correlations across frames in a growing co-visibility or keyframe graph and iteratively revises both **per-pixel depth** and **camera pose**. The DBA layer then projects those revisions onto geometrically consistent solutions, and the whole loop remains differentiable. Classical systems in the context, especially **DSO**, also rely on bundle adjustment concepts, but they do not use this learned recurrent refinement mechanism. The corpus explicitly notes that the underlying BA idea is conceptually similar to DSO, but in DROID-SLAM the **feature extractor and update rule are learned**. [5][6][7]

Another major difference is **robustness and scope**. DROID-SLAM is described as being **substantially more robust** than classical baselines such as **ORB-SLAM** and **DSO**, especially on challenging benchmarks including **EuRoC, TUM-RGBD, TartanAir, and ETH3D**, where classical systems are said to suffer catastrophic tracking failures more often. It is also trained end-to-end on **monocular video**, but at test time it can ingest **monocular, stereo, and RGB-D** streams to improve accuracy. [5]

In the broader literature represented here, DROID-SLAM is positioned as the **modern learned counterpart** to classical visual odometry and SLAM. The repository is described as a **de-facto deep SLAM baseline**, and later neural-implicit SLAM systems such as **NICE-SLAM, Co-SLAM, and Point-SLAM** benchmark against it. So compared with classical SLAM, DROID-SLAM is not simply a better-engineered version of the same pipeline: it is a shift toward **learned front ends and learned iterative refinement**, while still preserving the geometric optimization core that classical SLAM systems relied on. [1][5][6]

question: What role does Structure from Motion play in modern 3D reconstruction pipelines?
answer:
## Role of Structure from Motion in Modern 3D Reconstruction Pipelines

Structure from Motion (SfM) plays a **foundational upstream role** in many modern 3D reconstruction pipelines by estimating **camera poses** before reconstruction or rendering models are trained. In the context provided, NeRF-style methods assume that accurate camera poses are already available, and a structure-from-motion pipeline is the process used to provide them. COLMAP is the clearest example: it is described as a core preprocessing dependency that computes camera poses from real data and serves as a standard SfM pipeline in these workflows.

SfM also provides the **initial scene geometry** that downstream methods build on. Its outputs include a **sparse point cloud**, which is used to seed optimization in 3D Gaussian Splatting pipelines. The training pipeline for Gaussian Splatting takes a sparse COLMAP SfM point cloud and densifies it into millions of anisotropic 3D Gaussians. More broadly, Structure From Motion is described as the standard offline stage that produces the sparse point cloud used to initialize reconstruction near the true surface.

At the same time, modern systems differ in how dependent they remain on SfM. Some methods, such as vanilla 3D Gaussian Splatting, still require **accurate per-image camera poses from an offline SfM stage**. In contrast, SLAM-oriented systems such as **MonoGS** move this pose estimation online: instead of relying on precomputed poses, they estimate camera motion directly from a live monocular stream while reconstructing the scene. This makes SfM less of a fixed preprocessing step and more of a capability that newer systems try to internalize.

SfM also appears as an important **geometric inductive bias** inside learned systems. DeepV2D embeds classical structure-from-motion as differentiable modules within a deep network, alternating motion and depth estimation to convergence. DROID-SLAM extends that iterative refinement idea into full SLAM with a differentiable Dense Bundle Adjustment layer. So in modern pipelines, SfM is not only a preprocessing tool but also a model for how geometry and pose estimation can be built into trainable architectures.

Finally, the context makes clear that SfM is highly useful but not perfect. COLMAP is both a required dependency and a source of reconstructed geometry in many workflows, yet its reconstruction process may fail to recover camera poses for some custom datasets. That means SfM remains central to modern 3D reconstruction, but it is also a practical bottleneck that newer feed-forward and online systems increasingly try to reduce or bypass.

question: What are the trade-offs between explicit primitives (like 3DGS) and implicit neural fields (like NeRF)?
answer:
## Trade-offs Between Explicit Primitives and Implicit Neural Fields

The core trade-off is **speed and practicality versus geometric structure and representation style**.

**Implicit neural fields** like **NeRF** represent a scene as a continuous function, typically a coordinate-based MLP that maps spatial location and viewing direction to color and opacity. This gives a very flexible scene model and a clean volumetric rendering formulation, but it is computationally expensive: NeRF optimization usually takes **hours to days per scene**, and rendering can range from **under a second to tens of seconds per image**, depending on resolution. The original NeRF setup also became the canonical baseline for later radiance-field work because of its 5D radiance-field MLP, positional encoding, and hierarchical volume sampling design. [1][2]

**Explicit primitive methods** like **3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)** instead represent the scene with **explicit anisotropic 3D Gaussian primitives** and render them with **fast differentiable rasterization**. This avoids costly sampling through empty space and enables **real-time rendering** while maintaining strong visual quality. In the provided context, 3DGS is described as a major alternative to NeRF on static-scene benchmarks, with at least **30 FPS at 1080p**, and as having beaten **Instant-NGP** on quality-at-FPS and training-time fidelity at 1080p. So the main advantage of explicit primitives is **speed**, especially for rendering and interactive use. [3][14]

A second trade-off is **view synthesis quality versus geometry quality**. The context describes 3DGS as excellent for **novel view synthesis**, but also as an **unstructured optimized primitive cloud** with many redundant primitives. Because of that, it is often considered **less suitable when accurate geometry is required**, and its output is described as difficult to convert into a mesh. In contrast, the implicit-surface branch of neural fields, especially methods like **NeuS**, is designed to recover cleaner, mesh-extractable geometry by representing shape as the **zero level set of a signed distance function (SDF)** and rendering it with an adapted volume-rendering formulation. NeuS is explicitly presented as a strong high-fidelity surface-reconstruction baseline, especially for sharp reconstructions. [15][18]

There is also a trade-off in **robustness and scene assumptions**. Vanilla 3DGS is described as brittle in **texture-less regions** and under **view-dependent lighting effects**, partly because its unstructured primitives do not provide strong shared support across views. The context also notes unresolved behavior under **fast camera motion** in SLAM-oriented extensions. Implicit surface methods have different weaknesses: methods in the NeuS line can degrade on **large, textureless, or sparsely captured scenes**, and NeuS itself is described as relatively **slow** and sensitive to capture quality. MonoSDF is presented as a follow-up precisely because pure multi-view implicit surface methods struggle in those harder settings. [7][15]

Finally, the two families differ in how the field has used them. The context frames **NeRF-style implicit fields** as the dominant earlier paradigm for 3D reconstruction and radiance fields, while **3DGS** marks a shift toward **explicit primitives** because it preserves strong image quality while making rendering much faster. At the same time, implicit methods remain important where **surface fidelity, signed-distance structure, and mesh extraction** matter most. So, within this corpus, the trade-off is not that one family fully replaces the other: **explicit primitives win on rendering speed and practical interactivity; implicit neural fields, especially SDF-based ones, remain stronger when the goal is structured, high-fidelity surface reconstruction**. [2][10][15]

question: What is differentiable rasterization and why does it matter for 3D Gaussian Splatting?
answer:
**Differentiable rasterization** is the rendering procedure that projects a scene’s **3D Gaussians** into screen space and composes them into an image in a way that still supports gradient-based optimization. In the 3D Gaussian Splatting pipeline, each Gaussian carries attributes such as position, covariance, opacity, and view-dependent color, and these projected Gaussians are rendered with a **tile-based, front-to-back** splatting process rather than ray-marching through an implicit volume. [9]

It matters because this rendering step is what makes **3D Gaussian Splatting trainable and fast at the same time**. Since the rasterization is differentiable, errors in the rendered image can be backpropagated to update each Gaussian’s parameters. That is how the method can optimize a scene representation directly from image reconstruction objectives while refining position, scale, rotation, opacity, and color. The original 3DGS paper combines this with a **closed-form, GPU-friendly backward pass** and a **visibility-aware tile rasterizer**, which is a major reason it can reach **real-time rendering at 1080p** while maintaining strong visual quality. [9]

It also matters because differentiable rasterization is a core reason 3DGS differs from **NeRF-style methods**. Instead of sampling many points along rays through empty space, 3DGS rasterizes explicit anisotropic primitives. This avoids much of the wasted computation associated with implicit-volume ray marching and is central to the efficiency advantage of splatting-based methods. [9]

At the ecosystem level, differentiable rasterization became a foundational interface for later work. The `gsplat` library re-implements the core differentiable rasterization of 3D Gaussians with explicit, documented gradients, and its math supplement details the forward and backward passes for projection, covariance propagation, alpha compositing, and gradients through opacity, color, position, scale, and rotation. That made it a common substrate for extensions such as **SuGaR**, **2D Gaussian Splatting**, dynamic Gaussian methods, and SLAM systems. [3][18]

question: What are the main open challenges in dynamic 3D reconstruction?
answer:
## Main Open Challenges

The biggest open challenge is **scale and realism of capture conditions**. In the corpus, dynamic reconstruction methods are reported mostly on **short clips**, typically with **a single deformable subject** under relatively controlled conditions. This is true for canonical deformable-NeRF systems such as **Nerfies**, its follow-up **HyperNeRF**, and also **Dynamic View Synthesis from Dynamic Monocular Video**. Even newer splatting-based methods such as **4D Gaussian Splatting** and **4D-Rotor Gaussian Splatting** are described as achieving real-time dynamic novel-view playback, but still evaluating on short captures rather than long, complex real-world videos. The open question is whether these methods work on **several-minute hand-held monocular captures** with **multiple moving subjects** and **changing illumination**. [2][11][18]

A second challenge is the **representation itself**. Much of the dynamic line relies on a **canonical representation plus deformation field**. That formulation works for elastic deformation, but the corpus notes that it struggles when there is **no single canonical state** covering the whole sequence, or when scenes contain **topological changes**, **abrupt motion**, or **appearing/disappearing content**. In the monocular setting, the ambiguity is worse: dynamic view synthesis from a single video is under-constrained, so methods need regularizers such as **3D scene flow consistency**, **depth-order constraints**, and **2D optical-flow losses** just to bias optimization toward plausible solutions. [9][18]

A third challenge is **time and memory scaling**. The corpus explicitly points out that the likely failure modes at longer durations are structural: deformation-network capacity is fixed at training time, and in **4DGS-class methods** the **per-Gaussian temporal trajectories grow linearly with primitive count**, while densification schedules were not designed for long, cluttered dynamic captures. **K-Planes** is described as the most aggressive method on duration because its factorization makes memory growth **linear in time rather than quadratic**, but even its public results remain on **minutes-or-less captures**. [2][11]

A fourth challenge is **robust multi-view consistency under difficult camera motion**. The corpus raises this as a separate open question: splatting-based systems assume relatively smooth trajectories, but it is unclear how well they hold up when the camera moves fast, introduces **motion blur**, or changes scale and exposure rapidly. The concern is that the same Gaussian may project very differently across adjacent frames, causing instability in tracking and reconstruction. The corpus notes that splatting-SLAM systems have not published the harder benchmark numbers that would settle this issue. [14]

Finally, there is a **benchmarking gap**. The corpus repeatedly says the field lacks the datasets and evaluations needed to resolve these questions cleanly. What is missing is a benchmark of **multi-minute monocular dynamic captures with calibrated ground truth**, plus **head-to-head comparisons** across methods such as **K-Planes, 4DGS, 4D-Rotor-GS, HyperNeRF, and Nerfies**, ideally with explicit ablations on capture length and operating conditions. Until such benchmarks exist, the field can show impressive short-clip results without proving robustness in real-world dynamic capture. [2][11]
